coredata.dc:title: Tuning the photocatalytic activity of graphene quantum dots via decorating the X% (Co3O4) as modern photocatalysts to produce ammonia
coredata.dc:creator: Mokhtari, A.
Khatamian, M.
coredata.dc:description: The crises related to energy and pollutants in the present century have led to the special look toward clean and sustainable energies. Material science plays a significant role in the way to achieve the world’s envisioned sustainability. This paper introduces an outstanding and facile route for decorating cobalt oxide (Co3O4) on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form the X% (Co3O4)/GQDs composites. The 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites are formed with the aid of ultrasound irradiation, followed by the pyrolysis of citric acid employing a bottom-up approach. Decoration of Co3O4 nanoparticles with various weight percentages of 5%, 10%, and 20% is uniformly performed into the GQDs with the size distribution of 4.4, 4.6, and 4.8 nm, respectively. The resultant composites are applied as the robust photocatalysts for the conversion of N2 to NH3. In this report, GQDs decorated by three ratios of 5%, 10%, and 20% of Co3O4 are formed to evaluate the effect of decorated Co3O4 weight percentage on the production of ammonia. In addition, the kinetics and the concentration of the produced ammonia are studied using these composites. Based on the comparative analysis, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst not only optimizes the production of ammonia related to 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts but also indicates the faster kinetics of ammonia production. These results can be attributed to the more uniform distribution and the lower size of nanoparticles in 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Co3O4 nanoparticles
NH3 photofixation
Photocatalyst
Ammonia production

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of 5% Co3O4/GQDS (A, B), 10% Co3O4/GQDS (C, D), and 20% Co3O4/GQDS (E, F) composites, the inset of Fig. 2 (A, C, and E) showing the corresponding histogram related to the size of NPs.
IR spectra of Co3O4, GQDS, 5% Co3O4/GQDS, 10% Co3O4/GQDS, and 20% Co3O4/GQDS samples.
DRS profiles related to in Co3O4, GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs (A) and the estimated band gap of Co3O4, GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs (B).
XRD patterns of 5% Co3O4/GQDS, 10% Co3O4/GQDS, and 20% Co3O4/GQDS composites.
EDS spectra of 5% Co3O4/GQDS and 10% Co3O4/GQDS composites.
Nitrogen isotherms for 5% Co3O4/GQDS (A), 10% Co3O4/GQDS (B), and 20% Co3O4/GQDS (C). The BJH curves corresponding to the 5% Co3O4/GQDS(D), 10% Co3O4/GQDS (E), and 20% Co3O4/GQDS (F).
The profiles related to the produced concentrations of ammonia after 150 min for GQDs, Co3O4, 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDS, 10% Co3O4/GQDS, and 20% Co3O4/GQDS samples.
The kinetics related to the production of ammonia after 150 min for GQDs, Co3O4, 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDS, 10% Co3O4/GQDS, and 20% Co3O4/GQDS samples.
The production mechanism of NH3 in the presence of X% (Co3O4)/GQDs.
The produced concentrations of NH3 (µmol/L) on the surface of 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts at various times.
The kinetics related to the production of NH3 on the surface of 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts at various times.
Comparison of the Activities Related to the Resultant composites with Previous Reports to Produce the Ammonia.

head.ce:title: Tuning the photocatalytic activity of graphene quantum dots via decorating the X% (Co3O4) as modern photocatalysts to produce ammonia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Inorganic Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physical Inorganic Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The crises related to energy and pollutants in the present century have led to the special look toward clean and sustainable energies. Material science plays a significant role in the way to achieve the world’s envisioned sustainability. This paper introduces an outstanding and facile route for decorating cobalt oxide (Co3O4) on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form the X% (Co3O4)/GQDs composites. The 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites are formed with the aid of ultrasound irradiation, followed by the pyrolysis of citric acid employing a bottom-up approach. Decoration of Co3O4 nanoparticles with various weight percentages of 5%, 10%, and 20% is uniformly performed into the GQDs with the size distribution of 4.4, 4.6, and 4.8 nm, respectively. The resultant composites are applied as the robust photocatalysts for the conversion of N2 to NH3. In this report, GQDs decorated by three ratios of 5%, 10%, and 20% of Co3O4 are formed to evaluate the effect of decorated Co3O4 weight percentage on the production of ammonia. In addition, the kinetics and the concentration of the produced ammonia are studied using these composites. Based on the comparative analysis, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst not only optimizes the production of ammonia related to 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts but also indicates the faster kinetics of ammonia production. These results can be attributed to the more uniform distribution and the lower size of nanoparticles in 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is the foundation of modern society. The environmental problems (e.g., global warming, climate change, and environmental pollutants) along with the heavy dependence of modern human life on different types of energies (e.g., fuel cells, ORR, water splitting, and so forth), agricultural, pharmaceutical, and petrochemical industries have created significant concerns in the present century [1–8]. Hence, the production of novel kinds of efficient materials with outstanding behaviors is a grand challenge for scientists. The recent advances in the field of materials science have opened new windows toward various topics [9–13]. In this context, QDs (quantum dots) have emerged as the fascinating kinds of zero-dimensional (0D) networks. These materials offer exceptional optical behavior with small sizes of 2–10 nm. Among QDs, GQDs (graphene quantum dots) have appeared as state-of-the-art materials [14–26]. These unique materials display unique properties such as a tunable band gap, favorable biocompatibility, extraordinary luminescence, excellent solubility, and low toxicity. Consequently, these attractive materials have a high potential for application in various areas of research (e.g., sensors, batteries, solar cells, fluorescent agents, light-emitting, etc.).

Overall, two distinct procedures have been reported for the production of GQDs: (1) Bottom-Up and (2) Top-Down [27]. The former, procedure involves building up from atomic scale to nanoscale while the Bottom-Up approach is commonly used for aromatic molecules such as fullerene (C60) and C6H6 since the size and shape of GQDs will be controlled during their growth. a most recent research, the exfoliation and decomposition of carbon derivatives (e.g., carbon fiber, carbon black, graphite powder, etc.) led to the production of GQDs [27]. These processes can be performed utilizing various kinds of routes (e.g., Photo-Fenton reaction, electrochemical, hydrothermal, plasma, or acidic oxidation).

Ammonia (NH3) plays a pivotal role in developing agricultural and modern industrial products. Therefore, the production of ammonia is an urgent need to the development of various industries. The traditional N2 fixation for creating NH3 was industrially performed by the Haber-Bosch process [28]. In this process, N2 and H2 gases react with each other to create NH3 at the temperature of 573–773 K and pressure of 15–25 MPa. This process not only generates large amounts of carbon dioxide as a by-product but also involves an energy-intensive process. Hence, creating mild favorable conditions is indispensable to the fixation process of N2 to NH3. In recent years, the photocatalytic-related processes have emerged as highly promising for converting N2 to NH3. Compared with Haber-Bosch way, the photofixation of N2 to NH3 is performed without CO2 emissions along with operation security under ambient pressure and temperature, and low energy consumption driven by light source. In addition, it is demonstrated that metallic oxides can be applied as efficient photocatalysts in converting N2 to NH3. Hence, metallic oxides are considered efficient photocatalysts to produce ammonia [29–31].

Apart from the mentioned discussions, it has been demonstrated that the physicochemical characteristics of GQDs (e.g., optical properties and chemical reactivity) can be effectively improved by the addition of a semiconductor or a heteroatom [32]. This means that the composites developed using metallic oxides and GQDs can significantly enhance the efficiency in diverse applications. For example, Luo and co-workers reported the production of a tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs composite for advanced asymmetric supercapacitors. They proved that this NiCo2O4@GQDs composite depicts an extraordinary specific capacitance of 1242 F g−1 at 30 A g−1 in KOH (2.0 M) electrolytes owing to the synergistic effect between the conductive GQDs and tremella-like NiCo2O4 [33]. Fei and colleagues reported the production of the GQDs modified Bi2WO6 (GQD/Bi2WO6) composites to photocatalytic N2 fixation [32].

In this manuscript, a facile and valuable procedure is introduced to form the GQDs-based photocatalysts. Three 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites were formed with the aid of ultrasound irradiation, followed by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). The Co3O4 NPs with various weight percentages is decorated successfully onto GQDs to consider the effect of decorated weight percentage on the production of ammonia. In addition, the kinetics and the concentration of produced ammonia were studied by these composites. In this research, the novel composites were fabricated utilizing a facile and outstanding way. For the first time, these resultant composites were utilized as efficient photocatalysts in converting N2 to NH3 and showed the exceptional efficiency to produce ammonia.

In order to implement the research, CA (C6H8O7) (Mw = 192.12 g/mol and purity = 99.5–100.5%), cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O) (Mw = 249.08 g/mol and purity = 99.0–101.0%), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (Mw = 40 g/mol and purity= ≥ 99.0%) were purchased from Merck chemical company and used without further purification.

Diffractograms of 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites were recorded at 2θ = 10–90 with a Japanse-made Rigaku D/max-3C using Cu Ka radiation. Detailed examination of morphology and functional group of resultant photocatalysts was carried out by recording transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) with the models of F20S-TWIN 200KV and Thermos Nicolet 360, respectively. Surface area and pores size of the resultant composites were estimated by recording ad-/desorption of N2 utilizing Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) models. UV–vis spectra (with a model of Scinco S4100 spectrophotometer) relevant to all composites were conducted at RT (room temperature) in the wavelength region of 300–1000 nm.

Cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate with a concentration of 0.1 M was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water. Next, the pH level of the environment was tuned to 8 via NaOH (0.1 M), and the achieved suspension was kept under stirring for two hours at 353 K [34]. In the following, the resulting Co3O4 was filtered through centrifugation and cleaned many times with distilled water until it reached a neutral pH. The resulting Co3O4 was dried at 333 K in an oven for 300 min, followed by the calcination process of obtained precipitate in a furnace at 773 K for a time duration of 7200 s.

Three kinds of X% (Co3O4) /GQDs photocatalysts were fabricated utilizing the carbonization of CA as follows. In summary, the certain amounts of CA and Co3O4 (5%, 10%, and 20%) were separately dispersed in distilled water to form a homogeneous suspension. Then, each produced suspension was exposed to high-power ultrasound irradiation for 5400 s. Afterward, all produced suspensions were put separately into a crucible and were heated at 473 K for a time duration of 0.5 h. After cooling, the pH of each suspension was adjusted to 7 by adding NaOH solution with a dropwise concentration of 0.25 mol/L under vigorous stirring. This observation was corroborated by the pyrolysis of CA to form the GQDs. Eventually, the solvent was evaporated at 373 K for one day to create the final products. After this period, a dark grey color was observed on products.

The efficiencies related to three photocatalysts in the production of NH3 were investigated in a photoreactor utilizing a LED lamp with an irradiation power of 50 W at 298 K. The reaction mixture included 0.05 gr of nano-photocatalyst and 50 Âµl of methanol (as a sacrificing agent) in 50 mL of water, in which the gas (N2) flow rate was 100 mL/min. Spectrophotometry method was used to examine the product quantitatively. To this end, sampling was performed by taking 1.5 mL of the reaction mixture. Then, the samples were centrifuged, and the supernatant was separated to measure the amount of ammonia. After that, a few drops of Nessler's reagent were added to the supernatant, which was then analyzed by a spectrophotometer. The peak observed at 425 nm was ascribed to the ammonia. Each test related to the concentration and kinetics of NH3 was repeated three times. The final data were reported by averaging the three trials.

More recently, the synthesis and development of GQD-based composites have been spotlighted as an emergency topic worldwide. Hence, our research team focused on the production of a valuable and facile route to form GQD-based composites. Three photocatalysts with various weight percentages of Co3O4 were formed with the aid of ultrasound irradiation, followed by the pyrolysis of CA. The pyrolysis of CA at 200 ℃ under a time duration of 0.5 h produced a dark grey product of GQDs.

TEM images were taken to analyze the morphology of the resultant composites. To this end, two pictures with various resolutions (100 and 50 nm) were recorded (Fig. 1 ) for each composite. The represented pictures for all composites display the scattered Co3O4 NPs on the surface of GQDs. As such, it can be readily observed that the distribution of NPs on the GQDs in 5% Co3O4/GQDs composite is performed more uniformly in comparison to 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites. Based on this characteristic, it is predicted that the efficiency of 5% Co3O4/GQDs composite for the photocatalytic processes is more than other composites.

The inset of Fig. 1(A, C, and E) reflects the histogram relevant to the mean diameter of Co3O4 NPs anchored on the GQDs-based composites. Based on TEM images and corresponding histograms, Co3O4 NPs were uniformly scattered on the GQDs with a mean diameter of 4.4, 4.6, and 4.8 nm for 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites, respectively.

We compared the IR spectra relevant to Co3O4, GQDs, and X% (Co3O4)/GQDs samples to characterize the main functional groups in the X% (Co3O4)/GQDs photocatalyst. The IR spectra of Co3O4, GQDs, and X% (Co3O4)/GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 .

The presence of asymmetric (1680 cm−1) and symmetric (1315 cm−1) absorptions related to the –COOH groups in the X% (Co3O4)/GQDs samples imply the production of compounds containing the carboxyl groups [35]. Moreover, GQDs reflect the bands of vibration relevant to O–H and C–H at around 3330 and 2950 cm−1, respectively [35]. Interestingly, no absorptions are characterized for an aromatic sample (e.g., skeletal vibration of C = C (1450–1650 cm−1) and aromatic rings (C–H, 3000–3100 cm−1) in these IR spectra [35]. These observations corroborate the absence of any aromatic characteristic in each 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composite. The absorption band related to Co3O4 appeared in four IR profiles relevant to Co3O4 and X% (Co3O4)/GQDs composites at around 500–700 cm−1, suggesting the presence of Co3O4 in each sample.

The Eg values related to Co3O4, GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs (Fig. 3 ) were obtained through the relationship explained in previous reports. Based on the performed calculations, Eg values for Co3O4, GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs compounds are 2.4, 3.05, 2.57 and 2.35 eV, respectively.

Diffractograms related to all composites were carefully analyzed to explore the elements and crystal structure of 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites (Fig. 4 ). In each diffractogram, at around 20 to 25˚, a broad peak appeared. This assigned reflection with miller indices (002) confirms the existence of GQDs in each composite [27]. As such, the 10% Co3O4/GQDs and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites exhibits the same reflections relevant to Co3O4 at angles of 30.63˚, 36.17˚, 44.25˚, 55.40˚, 58.72˚ and 64.84˚. This pattern confirms a cubic crystal structure of Co3O4 (PDF# 96–900-5896) for these composites.

Fig. 5 presents the EDS spectra of the 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and the results are summarized in Table inset of Fig. 5(A and B). It can be observed that the EDS spectrum is indicative of the presence of carbon, oxygen, and cobalt in these 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs composites.

The surface area relevant to the resultant photocatalysts was calculated via N2 ad-/desorption (Fig. 6 (A-C)). The achieved profiles for 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts display the same isotherm (type III), while the profile related to 20% Co3O4/GQDs is a type IV isotherm [36]. The N2 plots related to the 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts show surface areas of 8.33, 8.63, and 15.41 m2/g, respectively.

Likewise, the BJH profile related to each photocatalyst was employed for exploring the pore size of the generated photocatalyst (Fig. 6(D-F)). Based on the represented plots, two kinds of pores are identified in the BJH plots for 5% Co3O4/GQDs and 10% Co3O4/GQDs composites: 1) the pores of less than 2 nm (micropore) and 2) the pores of 2–50 nm (mesopore) [29,30]. However, the 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst shows three types of pores in the BJH plot: 1) micropore, 2) mesopore, and 3) pores larger than 50 nm (macropore) [37,38]. Characterization of the X% (Co3O4)/GQDs composites was completely performed utilizing the XRD, nitrogen isotherm, IR spectra, EDS, Eg values, and TEM, analyses.

It is corroborated that the GQDs can effectively promote the production of NH3. Hence, we synthesized three GQD-based photocatalysts for the conversion of N2 to NH3. The tests related to N2 photofixation to produce NH3 were conducted at a time interval of 150 min in a photoreactor under ambient conditions [32]. Fig. 7 and Table 1 compare the efficiencies of 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts for the production of NH3.

As illustrated, among these compounds, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst reveals a more regular trend in comparison with other photocatalysts for producing NH3 over durations of 30 to 150 min. As such, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst shows the best efficiency for producing NH3 in comparison to other photocatalysts. The optimal time for the production of NH3 is about 150 min with a concentration of 3801 µmol/L of NH3.

One of the pivotal issues in the conversion of N2 to NH3 is the kinetics related to this reaction [31]. We examined the production rates of NH3 at a time duration of 150 min. Fig. 8 and Table 2 display the kinetics associated with the generation of NH3 in the presence of 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts.

As presented, all photocatalysts offer the maximum velocity for producing NH3 at a time interval of 0–30 min of the reaction progress (0 ≤ t /min ≤ 30). After that, the production rate of NH3 decreases owing to the reverse process. Additionally, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst delivers faster kinetics for producing NH3 and N2H4 than those reported for other photocatalysts. Based on the comparative analysis, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst not only optimizes the production of ammonia related to other photocatalysts but also indicates the faster kinetics for ammonia production.

In addition, the reported activities for some photocatalysts to produce NH3 were compared in Table 3 . This comparison depicts an outstanding activity of 2% Co3O4/GQDs, 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalysts (this work) relative to other photocatalysts.

Since the energy related to the conduction band for Co3O4 (0.41 eV) is more positive compared to the GQDs (-0.91 eV) [39,40], Hence, it seems that the production mechanism of NH3 in the presence of Co3O4/GQDs can be according to the Fig. 9 .

Both GQDs and Co3O4 NPs are aroused during the visible light irradiation. This process leads to the electron transition from the conduction band of GQDs to the conduction band of Co3O4. As such, the hole transfer will occur in the opposite direction between valence bands of GQDs and Co3O4. Therefore, the produced electron and hole are placed on the surface of Co3O4 and GQDs, respectively. In this condition, the electron-hole pair is effectively split to produce NH3. This synergetic effect between Co3O4 NPs and GQDs increases the production efficiency of NH3 in the presence of composites relative to pure Co3O4 and GQDs.

In this paper, a simple and outstanding route was introduced for decorating Co3O4 on GQDs to form the X% (Co3O4)/GQDs composites. Three resultant composites were carefully formed with the aid of ultrasound irradiation employing a bottom-up approach, followed by the pyrolysis of CA. Decoration of Co3O4 NPs with various weight percentages of 5%, 10%, and 20% was uniformly carried out on GQDs with size distribution of 4.4, 4.6, and 4.8 nm, respectively. Three weight ratios of 5% Co3O4/GQDs, 10% Co3O4/GQDs, and 20% Co3O4/GQDs composites were applied as the photocatalysts for the conversion of N2 to NH3 to examine the effect of decorated Co3O4 weight percentage on the production of NH3. Furthermore, the kinetics and the concentration of produced ammonia on the surface of X% (Co3O4)/GQDs composites were studied. Based on the comparative analysis, the 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst not only optimizes the production of ammonia (3801 µmol/L) in comparison to 10% Co3O4/GQDs (3824.3 µmol/L), and 20% Co3O4/GQDs (2217.4 µmol/L) photocatalysts but also exhibits faster kinetics for ammonia production. These results can be attributed to the more uniform distribution and the lower size of nanoparticles in 5% Co3O4/GQDs photocatalyst. For the first time, in this research, the novel composites were prepared utilizing a facile and outstanding way. These novel composites were utilized as efficient photocatalysts in N2 photofixation process and showed the exceptional efficiency to produce ammonia.
A. Mokhtari: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. M. Khatamian: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Nat. Energy

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive enzyme-free electrochemical sensor based on a rod-shaped bimetallic MOF anchored on graphene oxide nanosheets for determination of glucose in huangshui
